description: Law governing cheques, promissory notes, and bills of exchange, especially cheque bounce (dishonour) cases under Section 138.

sections.title: Dishonour of Cheque (Cheque Bounce)
sections.text: Where any cheque drawn by a person on an account maintained by him with a banker for payment of any amount of money to another person from out of that account for the discharge of any debt or other liability is returned by the bank unpaid, either because of the amount of money standing to the credit of that account is insufficient to honour the cheque or that it exceeds the amount arranged to be paid from that account by an agreement made with that bank, such person shall be deemed to have committed an offence.
sections.simplified: If someone gives you a cheque and it bounces (due to insufficient funds or other reasons), the person who issued the cheque has committed a criminal offence under Section 138. You can file a case against them.
sections.punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to twice the cheque amount, or both

sections.title: Steps Before Filing Cheque Bounce Case
sections.text: The payee must: (a) present the cheque within its validity period (3 months), (b) give a written demand notice to the drawer within 30 days of receiving the bounce memo from bank, (c) if the drawer fails to make payment within 15 days of receiving the notice, (d) file a complaint to the Magistrate within 30 days of expiry of the 15-day notice period.
sections.simplified: To file a bounced cheque case: (1) Present cheque within 3 months, (2) After it bounces, send legal notice within 30 days, (3) Wait 15 days for payment, (4) If no payment, file a complaint in court within 30 days. Missing any deadline can weaken your case.

sections.title: Presumption in Favour of Holder
sections.text: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability.
sections.simplified: In cheque bounce cases, the law presumes in your favour — it is assumed the cheque was given for a genuine debt unless the accused proves otherwise. This makes it easier for the complainant to win.

sections.title: Power of Court to Try Cases Summarily
sections.text: All offences under Section 138 shall be tried by a Judicial Magistrate of the first class or by a Metropolitan Magistrate and the provisions of summary trial shall apply.
sections.simplified: Cheque bounce cases are tried by Magistrate courts using summary procedures, which are faster than regular trials. Some courts have been designated as exclusive cheque bounce courts for even faster resolution.

sections.title: Interim Compensation
sections.text: In every case where a complaint is filed under Section 138, the court may at any time during the pendency of the case direct the drawer of the cheque to pay interim compensation to the complainant in a summary trial or summons case, not exceeding twenty per cent of the cheque amount.
sections.simplified: During the trial, the court can order the accused to pay you up to 20% of the cheque amount as interim compensation, even before the final judgment. This provides partial relief while the case is ongoing.
